You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

<!--
MAINTAINER NOTES (stripped before injection; free context)

This file is intentionally short. If it starts approaching 200 lines, split
topic-specific rules into `.claude/rules/*.md` and reference them here.

Do not duplicate DATA_CONTRACT.md content — @-import it. The contract is
authoritative; this file is orientation.

# Activity Log — 2021-12-30

**Case:** [[cases/kamden-bland-mva-6-3-21/kamden-bland-mva-6-3-21|Kamden Bland MVA 6/3/21]]

## 16:37:19

Per CP: Client offered $2500.00 - JC has been conversing w/Guardian about photos of facial scar - Guardian lost photos when phone broke

## 16:35:34

Per CP: 11/05/2021 Hi Aaron,

Thank you for providing me documents along with your demand for settlement to William Bland, Kamden Bland, Barbie Bland, Donna Mozee, and Carolyn Mozee. I have reviewed the documents that you’ve provided and I do need some additional information on a few of them.

William Bland: Noting William’s scarring I would like to present a full limits offer for him of $100,000. I have already paid $5,000 med pay coverage directly. This is all the available funds for William.

Kamden Bland: His medical bills indicate a reduction from Medicaid. Please advise what the Medicaid lien is if they’ve presented one. Our policy indicates that we can pay med pay to whomever we deem appropriate, including the providers. Noting the Medicaid reductions it appears that the owed meds are less than the full med pay policy limits so that cannot be paid upfront to your firm. I can pay your firm $3,703.22 for you to pay the providers, so I can pay the providers directly if you’d like. I will await your decision on that. For Kamden’s soft tissue claim, I will present an offer of $2,500.

Barbie Bland: After reductions it appears that Barbie owes $10,262.20. Additionally, we have paid our $5,000 med pay limits directly and that leaves $5,262.20 unpaid. For Barbie’s soft tissue claim, I will present an offer of $2,500 for a total of $7,762.20

Donna Mozee: Medicare and Medicaid are both listed, please provide copies of their liens for confirmation of what billed meds are owed. I have already paid $5,000 in med pay limits directly so I will need the additional liens to determine what remains unpaid. I intend to present an offer of $2,500 + remaining unpaid meds once that’s determined.

Carolyn Mozee: Medicare is listed, please provide copies of their lien for confirmation of what billed meds are owed. I have already paid $5,000 in med pay limits directly so I will  need the additional liens to determine what remains unpaid. I intend to present an offer of $2,500 + remaining unpaid meds once that’s determined.

Please forward the additional information requested.

    17|
    18|# Negotiation After Demand
    19|
    20|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Post-Demand Sequence
    25|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    26|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    27|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    28|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    29|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    30|
    31|### Client Rejection Pattern
    32|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    33|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    34|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    35|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    36|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    37|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    38|
    39|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    40|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    41|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    42|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    43|
    44|### Communication Challenges
    45|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    46|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    47|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    48|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    53|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    54|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    55|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY, negotiation phase), one of two BI carriers (a state mutual) settled quickly after demand, while the second carrier (a regional insurer) remained in active negotiation. The adjuster from the second carrier returned a voicemail indicating willingness to discuss the claim. This demonstrates a split-settlement pattern: when multiple BI carriers are involved, individual carriers may settle at different times, creating a phased resolution. The SOL countdown (180 days noted in an automated alert) remained active on the open claim, adding urgency to the second negotiation track.
    56|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the case bypassed the demand phase and moved directly from treatment to litigation due to disputed liability. During litigation, defense counsel offered to "negotiate 50/50." The attorney discussed this with the client, explaining the case issues and defense counsel's proposal. This confirms that in disputed-liability cases, negotiation may occur within the litigation context rather than following the standard demand-offer-counter sequence.
    57|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the negotiation phase had a unique twist: the BI carrier tendered policy limits (25/50) within one day of the demand being sent, but settlement could not proceed because the limits had to be split among four injured plaintiffs represented by different firms. The negotiation shifted from firm-vs-insurer to inter-counsel allocation. The attorney emailed all co-plaintiffs' counsel to negotiate distribution. One attorney was slow to provide medical bills, stalling the process for months. The firm updated the client regularly but could only say "we are still waiting." When inter-counsel agreement was not reached, the case moved to litigation. This shows a novel negotiation pattern: the insurer has already paid limits, but the money cannot be distributed without all parties' agreement.
    58|- In Case R (auto accident, minor injury — airbag rash, DOI 4/2023), a $5,000 demand was sent to the BI carrier. The carrier responded with a $500 "nuisance offer." The firm refused to negotiate further until the defendant had been deposed. After the defendant's deposition (3/2025 — nearly 2 years after the accident), the negotiation was expected to resume. This demonstrates a pattern where the firm deliberately pauses negotiation to build evidentiary leverage through litigation discovery, rather than accepting lowball offers on minor injury cases.
    59|- In Case S (auto accident, government transit defendant, DOI 9/2021), a BI settlement offer of $15,000 was received from a government risk management entity during active litigation. The firm was simultaneously corresponding with the government entity's attorney about the case. The negotiation during litigation pattern — where offers continue even after complaint filing — is confirmed by this case. Additionally, multiple liens (Medicare, Humana at $1,513, and health insurance) had to be resolved in parallel with the negotiation, complicating the settlement math on a government-defendant case.
    60|- In 4 cases from this batch (DOIs mid-2025 to late-2025, KY), demand was sent via fax/email to BI adjusters with attached records/bills, and the paralegal followed up requesting offer status. In one case, the firm demanded "policy limits" and attached a narrative report as supplemental support. The adjuster responded but the offer details were not yet visible. In another, both sides denied liability (rental vehicle driver), with the paralegal reporting to the attorney "both sides are denying liability on this. Not sure how to proceed" — demonstrating that disputed liability can stall negotiation entirely. In a third case, the firm sent demand to the BI adjuster and then followed up 2 weeks later asking to "confirm received" — the follow-up cadence of 2 weeks post-demand is a consistent pattern.
    61|- In a negotiation-phase case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was discovered to be incarcerated (in county jail) during active negotiation.
    62|- In 4 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-9/2025, negotiation phase), post-demand patterns confirmed: (1) In one case, the BI adjuster reviewed the PIP log after receiving the demand and found discrepancies: "what PIP has paid is significantly higher than the records from the demand show" — the adjuster requested additional records before making an offer. The firm provided the records and the adjuster responded with "increased offers for your clients" (companion case with coordinated offers). This confirms that adjusters use PIP payment data as a cross-check against demand records, and discrepancies delay offers. (2) In a multi-carrier case (2 BI carriers + 1 PIP), the firm sent demands to each BI carrier independently and followed up 3 days later requesting receipt confirmation. One carrier (Progressive) had not responded after 2 follow-ups over 2 weeks. (3) In one case, the demand was sent to National General BI via fax and email. No response was documented in the activity logs over 5+ months post-demand — confirming the "black hole" pattern where some carriers simply do not acknowledge demands. (4) In a companion case pair, the demand follow-up was "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" — a standardized Filevine task auto-generated upon demand dispatch.
    63|
    64|## Agent Guidance
    65|
    66|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    67|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    68|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    69|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    70|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    71|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    72|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    73|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    74|
    75|## Pitfalls
    76|
    77|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    78|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    79|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    80|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    81|
    82|### Batch 2 Evidence (30 additional cases)
    83|
    84|- In 29 cases from this batch, the standard demand-to-offer sequence was observed: demand sent, 2-week follow-up, initial offer received. Initial offers ranged from very low ($250 on a minor case, $1,500 on a soft-tissue case) to near-policy-limits ($41K, $100K policy tender). The typical time from demand to initial offer was 2-4 weeks when adjusters were responsive.
    85|- In a soft-tissue case (DOI 3/2024), the initial BI offer was $1,500. The firm obtained client authority to negotiate but "not pull trigger" without final approval. A new adjuster was assigned mid-negotiation, requiring file re-review. The adjuster ultimately offered $2,500-$3,000 range. The client was "iffy" on $3,000 but needed rental reimbursement resolved first — showing how ancillary PD/rental issues can delay BI settlement.
    86|- In a case (DOI 4/2022), the carrier's demand response included specific defense arguments: "can't place any value on the future medical expenses... she has not treated in almost two years... already treating for her knee injury when the accident occurred." The offer was "exclusive of PIP" with conditions: indemnity/hold harmless, no admission, confidentiality, complete release. This demonstrates sophisticated adjuster evaluation on pre-existing condition cases.
    87|- In a case (DOI 6/2024), after a counter of $10,500, the carrier responded: "It appears we are very far apart... extending our top and final offer of $4,000, exclusive of PIP, inclusive of all other liens." This "top and final" language is a common carrier tactic to shut down further negotiation.
    88|- "LBF" (Last Best and Final) is used extensively internally — the firm obtains client "authority to negotiate to LBF" before engaging. In 6+ cases, the pattern was: receive offer, contact client for authority, negotiate to LBF, present final number for client acceptance.
    89|- In 2 cases, the adjuster proactively called to follow up on whether the client had accepted the offer — showing adjusters track response timing and may push for closure.
    90|- In a case involving prior counsel (DOI 6/2022), the firm had to obtain PIP ledger urgently to "confirm SOL" before proceeding, adding SOL verification as a prerequisite to negotiation when taking over cases.
    91|- Automated Filevine task "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" was observed in 10+ cases, confirming standardized post-demand workflow.
    92|- In 3 cases, the attorney personally called the adjuster to negotiate after the paralegal's initial contact, confirming attorney involvement is triggered when initial offers are far below case value.
    93|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    94|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    95|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    96|
    97|### Batch 3 Evidence (15 additional cases from batch 1)
    98|
    99|- In a litigated MVA case (DOI 8/2019, KY, Nelson Circuit Court), a complete negotiation sequence was documented: demand sent 3/2024 -> defense counsel responded with $6,000 offer (inclusive of liens excl. PIP, with hold harmless/indemnity + agreed order of dismissal) -> firm countered at $20,000 -> defense raised to $6,500 -> firm countered at $13,000 -> defense raised to $7,000 -> attorney predicted they could reach $10,000 -> defense LBF at $7,500. Client rejected the LBF, citing ongoing neck issues and the 5+ year duration. This case shows the classic narrowing-gap pattern: defense starts at 30% of demand and slowly moves up, while the firm reduces demand by roughly 35% each round.
   100|- In a companion MVA case (DOI 4/2024, KY), the negotiation was resolved through attorney-to-adjuster phone call: initial offer $3,000, settled at $4,500. The attorney obtained authority from the client's POA holder (client was nonverbal/disabled). This confirms that for cases with POA representatives, the authority-to-settle process must go through the legal representative.
   101|- In a UM claim (DOI 9/2023, KY), the adjuster made an initial offer of $4,350 but was missing billing. After resending bills, the firm made repeated "calm" (called and left message) attempts over 3 months (18+ call attempts logged). LBF was $8,000. This case demonstrates extreme adjuster delay in responding — the firm logged "calm" nearly daily for weeks without response.
   102|- In an MVA case (DOI 11/2024, KY), the adjuster emailed a detailed counter: "$7,500 counter demand... We are able to extend an offer of $6,031... You advised that if we can get to $7,000 we can get this done." This shows adjusters documenting the entire phone negotiation in follow-up emails — creating a paper trail of verbal commitments.
   103|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), a prior demand existed at $15K and the carrier had an existing offer. The firm sent an EvenUp-generated demand and received a $21,670.50 initial offer. Client authorized "pull trigger at LBF." Settled at $40,000. This EvenUp demand achieved roughly 2x the initial offer, suggesting AI-generated demands may produce higher initial offers from carriers.
   104|- In a case (DOI 11/2023, KY), the firm received an initial offer of $40,502 on $100K policy limits. Settled at $50,000. The settlement at 50% of policy limits after initial offer at 40% shows limited negotiation headroom on higher-limit policies.
   105|- In an MVA case (DOI 8/2024, KY), the firm sent demand and received an initial offer of $4,000 within one month. Client was unreachable for weeks (phone not working, texts wouldn't go through, emails sent). Client eventually accepted at $4,900 "top offer." This confirms that client unreachability during negotiation can result in accepting lower offers due to inability to coordinate counter-strategy.
   106|- In a multi-track case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the PIP adjuster (Shelter Insurance) called the firm repeatedly (5+ voicemails over months) asking whether to close the PIP file, whether a PIP demand would be sent, and requesting an updated Medicare lien. The firm did not return calls for extended periods. The adjuster escalated to threatening file closure. This is a pattern where firm non-responsiveness to PIP adjusters creates risk of losing PIP recovery.
   107|- Settlement values observed in this batch: $4,500 (companion case), $7,000 (settled at mediation), $9,500 (MVA), $10,000 (WC), $15,000 (MVA with med pay for lien), $40,000 (EvenUp demand), $50,000 (high-limit case). The range confirms that Kentucky PI settlements for standard MVA soft-tissue cases cluster between $4,000-$15,000, with higher values requiring extensive treatment or EvenUp demands.
   108|- In 7+ cases, the "settled inclusive" vs "settled exclusive of PIP/med pay" distinction was critical: carriers specified whether the settlement amount included or excluded PIP/med pay obligations. The default in Kentucky appears to be "exclusive of PIP" (settlement is new money on top of PIP payments), but some releases include language about PIP subrogation indemnity that creates post-settlement complications.
   109|- In 4 cases, the release signing process was itself a bottleneck: clients failed to sign releases promptly (one case had multiple vinesign resend attempts), and in one case the BI carrier had not received the signed release months after settlement, delaying PIP reimbursement to the carrier. The adjuster called repeatedly requesting the signed release.
   110|
   111|### Batch 3 Evidence (25 additional cases)
   112|
   113|- In 25+ cases from this batch, detailed negotiation sequences were documented with specific offer amounts. The typical negotiation pattern: demand sent -> initial offer at 30-50% of demand -> firm counters -> 1-3 rounds of back-and-forth -> settlement at 50-70% of original demand.
   114|- In a case (KY, BI claim), detailed negotiation notes showed: initial offer $22K -> firm countered at $35K -> carrier raised to $25K -> settled at $27K. This 3-round negotiation pattern is the most commonly observed sequence.
   115|- In a KY Farm Bureau BI case, the initial offer was $35,500 on what appears to be a higher-value case. The adjuster requested additional records (PCP records from Valley View Health) before continuing evaluation. The firm sent updated records, and the adjuster confirmed receipt — but the follow-up cadence extended to months rather than weeks when additional records were involved.
   116|- In a case with Allstate BI, the demand produced an initial offer of $9,000. The firm noted "2/20 calm" (called and left message) — the "calm" abbreviation for adjuster follow-up calls is used consistently across 10+ cases in this batch.
   117|- In a case involving a prior accident (client had a 2nd MVA after the subject accident), the adjuster refused to consider chiropractic treatment, saying they would only value "DOL treatment only" at $3,500. The adjuster cited the subsequent accident as the basis for excluding the chiropractic. This confirms that subsequent accidents are used as a defense to limit treatment valuation in the same way that prior accidents are.
   118|- In a KY MVA case, the demand was sent and the adjuster tendered policy limits ($25K) within 1-2 days, triggering the COOTS letter process for UIM claim. The demand-to-tender timeline of 1-2 days for clear-liability cases with significant treatment is now confirmed across multiple cases.
   119|- In a case with a nuisance offer ($650 on a demand), the client was unreachable (phone disconnected, email bounced back, Lexis search confirmed disconnected numbers). The firm eventually settled at $1,200 — suggesting that unreachable clients force the firm to accept lower offers.
   120|- In a companion case pair (same accident, guardian and minor), both demands were sent to the same UM adjuster. The adjuster made an initial offer of $3,234.95 for the minor and a separate evaluation for the adult. The firm demanded $22,000 for the minor and waited for the adult's demand to go out simultaneously. This batch-demand approach for companions remains consistent.
   121|- In a BI claim that settled, the adjuster sent a formal email: "settlement agreement was for $2,000 exclusive of PIP... I will need the release signed and returned to me prior to making payment." The requirement to return the signed release BEFORE payment is issued is a consistent carrier requirement across 10+ cases.
   122|- In a UIM case (same carrier as PIP), the UIM adjuster offered $21,000 exclusive of PIP after computing that "after PIP payment and tortfeasor payment, he has been compensated around $4,800." The UIM adjuster's math explicitly deducted prior PIP and BI payments from the UIM evaluation, confirming that UIM value is calculated as incremental over prior recovery.
   123|- In a case with pre-settlement funding, the funding company (Barrister Capital) proactively contacted the firm asking about the case status and whether "there is acceptance from at fault" and "Is PIP paying?" — demonstrating that funding companies actively monitor cases and create additional communication overhead.
   124|- In 5+ cases, the attorney obtained "authority to pull trigger" from the client before accepting offers. This language is used consistently to confirm client authorization for settlement acceptance.
   125|
   126|## Related
   127|
   128|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — the demand that precedes negotiation
   129|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the long negotiation phase
   130|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all offers require attorney evaluation
   131|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL may force a decision during negotiation
   132|
   133|### Batch 4 Evidence (55 settlement activity cases)
   134|
   135|- In 55 cases from this batch, the standard post-demand negotiation sequence was observed across multiple carriers (Progressive, State Farm, Allstate, Liberty Mutual, USAA, Canal, Safeco, Erie, Shelter). Settlement values ranged from $2,150 (minor soft-tissue case) to $38,000+ (multi-provider treatment with significant medicals).
   136|- In a premises liability case (DOI 8/2023, KY), a detailed counter-offer exchange was documented: initial offer $9,600 -> firm countered at $25,000 -> carrier raised to $7,600 -> firm countered at $15,000 -> settled at $15,000. This 4-round negotiation pattern confirms the narrowing-gap convergence at 50-70% of demand.
   137|- In a high-value MVA case (DOI 1/2024, KY), the initial offer was $29,000 from Kentucky Farm Bureau. The firm logged "2/20 calm" (called and left message) and subsequent notes showed the offer increased to $38,000. The CALM abbreviation for adjuster follow-up calls continues to be used consistently.
   138|- In a UM case (DOI 11/2022, KY), the negotiation involved escalation to the UM adjuster's supervisor when the initial adjuster was unresponsive. A new UM adjuster was then assigned with "demand review in progress." The authority pattern confirmed: the firm noted getting "authority at $5K but I think I can push the adjuster up a little bit."
   139|- In a soft-tissue MVA case (DOI 7/2022, KY), the negotiation was simple: initial offer received, the firm asked "If you can get to $2,500" and it settled at $2,150. This demonstrates that low-value cases have minimal negotiation rounds.
   140|- In a multi-plaintiff MVA case (DOI 10/2021, KY), the negotiation was complicated by multiple parties represented by different firms: the BI carrier had to coordinate with co-plaintiff counsel for split agreement. The client's paralegal noted "your claim is taking a bit longer due to the number of parties involved."
   141|- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2021, KY), a SOL near-miss was documented: the attorney emailed staff "You have to pay attention to these. We missed the Statute of Limitations" — the demand was sent as a "quick demand with what's available" to preserve the claim. The adjuster applied Robinson v. Bates to reduce medical specials, and the case settled quickly at $5,000.
   142|- In 36 cases, settlement checks were issued, confirming the pipeline from offer acceptance through release signing to check issuance. VineSign was used for release signing in 29 cases.
   143|- In companion case pairs (DOI 2/2024, 7/2023), offers were made for both companions simultaneously by the same adjuster, with separate settlement amounts. One adjuster offered $4,250 for both adult and minor companion.
   144|- In 4+ cases, the adjuster's formal settlement email included specific conditions: "exclusive of PIP subrogation claim," "inclusive of all other claims including Medicare/Medicaid," "hold harmless/indemnity." The "exclusive of PIP" language remains the standard in Kentucky settlements.

    17|
    18|# Settlement Disbursement Workflow
    19|
    20|> After a settlement amount is agreed upon, the case enters the disbursement workflow: the carrier sends release documents, the client signs the release, the carrier issues the settlement check, the firm deposits the check, resolves liens, and distributes the net recovery to the client. Each step in this chain can become a bottleneck, and the period between "settled" and "money in client's pocket" is a frequent source of client frustration.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Standard Disbursement Sequence
    25|1. **Settlement Agreement**: Verbal or email agreement on amount and terms
    26|2. **Release Preparation**: Carrier or defense counsel sends release documents with specific terms (inclusive/exclusive of PIP, hold harmless, indemnity language, confidentiality)
    27|3. **Release Signing**: Client signs release via VineSign (electronic signature) or in person. May require notarization for some carriers/court cases
    28|4. **Release Return**: Firm sends signed release back to carrier/defense counsel
    29|5. **Check Issuance**: Carrier issues settlement check (payable to client and firm jointly)
    30|6. **Check Receipt & Deposit**: Firm receives and deposits check (clearing time applies)
    31|7. **Lien Resolution**: Firm pays outstanding liens (health insurance, PIP subrogation, Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid, etc.)
    32|8. **Fee Calculation**: Attorney fee (typically 33.3% pre-litigation, 40% post-litigation) deducted
    33|9. **Client Disbursement**: Net amount distributed to client via check or wire
    34|10. **Case Closing**: File moved to Closed phase
    35|
    36|### Common Bottlenecks
    37|- **Client fails to sign release**: VineSign resends are common; some clients need multiple reminders
    38|- **Release terms disputed**: PIP subrogation indemnity language or hold harmless clauses may require negotiation between the firm and defense counsel
    39|- **Check not received**: Carriers may delay check issuance; firm must follow up
    40|- **Lien not finalized**: Health insurance lien amounts may change after settlement, requiring updated ledgers
    41|- **Client urgency**: Clients want their money immediately but liens must be resolved first
    42|
    43|### Release Terms Observed
    44|- "Exclusive of PIP/med pay" — settlement is new money, PIP is separate
    45|- "Inclusive of all liens" — settlement amount must cover lien payments
    46|- "Hold harmless and indemnity" — client indemnifies carrier against future lien claims
    47|- "Agreed Order of Dismissal" — for litigated cases, requires court filing
    48|- "Confidentiality" — some releases include non-disclosure terms
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the release was sent to the client via VineSign. The client did not see it. Staff asked Justin to resend. Client was told to check email. This pattern of VineSign non-receipt/non-awareness was repeated in multiple cases.
    53|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the settlement check (#7590) for over $22K was issued. The firm called the client to notify her; she requested the check be mailed rather than picked up. A tracking number was sent via email. This shows the client preference for check delivery varies and should be asked.
    54|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 4/2024, KY), faxed signed and notarized releases (3 releases for 3 plaintiffs) were sent to the adjuster. The adjuster confirmed receipt. However, the client called via translator saying she had 3 days to pay medical bills before collections and needed her settlement check. The firm explained the insurance check had not yet been received. This demonstrates the gap between "settled" and "check received" causes acute client distress.
    55|- In a case (DOI 7/2024, KY), the BI carrier (Travelers) settled the claim but the firm had not returned the signed release months later. The adjuster called repeatedly (5+ voicemails) requesting the release because the PIP carrier was requesting reimbursement and could not be paid until the release was signed. This shows release delays create cascading problems for carriers too.
    56|- In a multi-plaintiff case (DOI 10/2021, KY), defense counsel sent settlement checks but expressly asked "they not be negotiated until your clients signed the releases." The checks were cashed/deposited without releases being returned, prompting defense counsel to demand compliance. This is a serious compliance issue — settlement checks should not be deposited until releases are executed.
    57|- In a case (DOI 3/2023, KY), the settlement at mediation required releases from multiple parties. The release negotiation itself took months — defense counsel and the firm exchanged emails about PIP subrogation indemnity language that was present in one release but not a companion's release. The firm pushed back: "our pre-mediation demands clearly stated that any settlement would be exclusive of PIP or indemnity obligations."
    58|- In 5+ cases, the firm used ZipLiens to process health insurance lien resolution. After settlement, the firm contacted the lien holder (Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid via Conduent, Optum) to obtain final lien amounts and negotiate reductions before disbursement.
    59|- In a case (DOI 5/2022, KY), the BI settled for $7K and the firm then had to determine if a UIM claim was open/pending before fully closing the file, demonstrating that settlement of one claim track does not necessarily close the case.
    60|- Across 39 additional KY cases (batch 2/3), settlement check delivery patterns confirmed: (1) Settlement checks were delivered by client pick-up or Priority Mail, with tracking numbers provided via email. (2) In a PL case (restaurant franchise, DOI 8/2023), the settlement check was for $16,247.48 — the client checked with her spouse about whether to pick up or have it mailed, demonstrating the standard delivery preference inquiry. (3) Post-settlement defense counsel filings (Agreed Order of Dismissal, Notice of Settlement) were processed even after the client received their check — defense counsel may file court documents weeks after disbursement. (4) "Ready to disburse?" internal queries appeared across multiple cases — the firm's internal check on whether the settlement check was received and liens resolved before contacting the client. (5) In a WC/MVA hybrid case, the settlement mechanism was Form 110 (not a standard BI release), requiring DWC approval of attorney fees before disbursement — WC settlements follow a fundamentally different disbursement pathway. (6) Multiple cases showed the attorney explicitly asking "Have we received settlement check?" before authorizing disbursement, confirming the check-receipt-gates-disbursement pattern. (7) Release documents were sometimes signed by defense counsel on behalf of their client ("You may sign the AOD on my behalf"), streamlining the process.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. When settlement is agreed, immediately send the release to the client via VineSign
    65|2. Follow up within 48 hours if the client has not signed — call and explain the document
    66|3. Once the release is signed, return it to the carrier/defense counsel the same day
    67|4. Track the settlement check issuance — set a 14-day follow-up for check receipt
    68|5. Before disbursement, confirm ALL liens are resolved: health insurance, PIP subrogation, Medicaid, child support, pre-settlement funding
    69|6. Ask the client their preference for receiving the check (mail vs. pickup)
    70|7. Do NOT deposit settlement checks until all releases are fully executed and returned
    71|8. Send the client a clear disbursement statement showing: gross settlement, attorney fees, lien payments, expenses, and net to client
    72|9. For multi-plaintiff cases, coordinate release signing across all plaintiffs before disbursement
    73|
    74|## Pitfalls
    75|
    76|- Depositing settlement checks before releases are returned (creates legal exposure)
    77|- Not following up on VineSign non-response (releases sit unsigned for weeks)
    78|- Client distress during the "settled but no check yet" window (proactively communicate the timeline)
    79|- Release terms not matching the negotiated agreement (review release language carefully)
    80|- Not resolving liens before disbursement (may require holdback or supplemental payment)
    81|- Defense counsel sending checks before releases are signed (creates compliance risk)
    82|
    83|## Related
    84|
    85|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — the settlement agreement that precedes disbursement
    86|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens that must be resolved before disbursement
    87|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the disbursement window
    88|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP subrogation that complicates release terms
    89|
    90|### Batch 4 Evidence (36 disbursement cases)
    91|
    92|- In 36 cases from this batch, settlement check issuance was documented with specific check numbers and payment workflows.
    93|- In a case (DOI 11/2022, KY), the check-to-Conduent workflow was documented: "mailed check #7638 to Conduent - $3,047.28 on behalf of [Client]." When the first check was not received, a second check was mailed: "check #7685 (#2) mailed to Conduent." This confirms that lien payment checks require tracking and potential re-issuance.
    94|- In a case (DOI 8/2022, KY), the firm mailed a lien payment check directly to Conduent: "check #7488 for $1,250.67 on behalf of [Client]." The Conduent lien was negotiated down from $12,000+ to $1,250.67.
    95|- In 29 cases, VineSign was used for release signing, confirming universal VineSign usage for settlement documents. Release completion was logged with timestamps and signer details.
    96|- The release-before-check pattern continues: the BI carrier requires "release signed and returned to me prior to making payment" across all carriers observed.
    97|- Post-settlement provider payment was documented: funeral home providers calling months after settlement requesting remaining balance payment, confirming that post-settlement lien/provider issues can persist for months.
    98|", "total_lines": 97, "file_size": 10241, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Medical Records and Bills Collection
     3|slug: medical-records-collection
     4|phase: [phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [records, bills, medical, providers, hipaa, fax, email]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 144
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - pip-claims-management
    13|  - demand-timing-strategy
    14|  - provider-communication-patterns
    15|  - government-entity-records-collection
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
    49|- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
    50|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
    51|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
    52|- In a treatment-phase case (DOI 1/2026), the firm sent records/bills requests via fax to PIP carrier and directly to the provider (Starlite Chiropractic) within weeks of file setup. The task "Confirm all medical providers to date including PCP" was created ~7 weeks post-DOI, confirming the periodic provider verification workflow. A separate request was faxed to the provider asking to "Please forward the records/bills" — demonstrating the direct provider request pattern alongside PIP submission.
    53|- In a companion case (DOI 11/2025), records requests were sent to UofL Hospital and chiropractic providers for multiple family members. The PIP adjuster's detailed billing analysis (denying specific CPT codes per patient) revealed that the PIP carrier independently reviews medical records and determines which services were "not rendered or were labeled incorrectly" — adding a new dimension to records collection where the PIP carrier's records review may conflict with the firm's records.
    54|- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "unable to locate PR" (police report) and followed up with a faxed crash report request. The report was eventually received via fax. This is consistent with the pattern of police report unavailability early in the case, requiring multiple request methods (online search, then fax request).
    55|- In 9 additional small cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed and expanded: (1) In a medical malpractice case, records from a children's hospital system (3 departments: medical group, urology, women's and children's) were all received on the same day, approximately 5 weeks after intake — hospital systems with centralized records departments respond faster than private practices; (2) In a case with a single orthopedic provider, records/bills requests were sent via fax to the provider's main number, with a follow-up fax sent 2 weeks later — confirming the standard 14-day follow-up cadence; (3) In a file-setup-phase case with the client still actively treating at a chiropractic provider, no records request was sent — records requests are deferred until treatment completion; (4) Urgent care clinic records were obtained through direct email correspondence with the clinic's front desk staff (not a medical records department), with records attached to the email response — smaller clinics have less formal records processes; (5) One urgent care clinic provided records and billing in response to a PIP exhaustion inquiry, demonstrating that billing-related correspondence can serve as a records collection trigger; (6) In companion cases sharing a chiropractic provider, records/bills for both companions were requested and received independently, with one companion's records arriving the same day as the request (same-day turnaround) while the other companion's took several weeks.
    56|- Across 55 additional KY cases (batch 2/3, DOIs 2021-2024), records collection patterns confirmed and expanded: (1) KRS 422.317 defense: when a records vendor (ScanSTAT) sent an invoice for records, the firm responded that "Per Kentucky statute KRS 422.317 the first copy is free" — successfully getting the invoice closed. This statutory defense should be used with all KY records vendor invoices. (2) ChartSwap, EpicLink, and MRO are the three primary records retrieval platforms in use: ChartSwap for community hospitals and specialist providers, EpicLink for Norton/hospital systems, and MRO for UofL Health. (3) Radiology billing is frequently separate from hospital billing — the firm sent separate requests to "University of Louisville Hospital - Radiology" for bills distinct from hospital records. (4) Companion cases with shared providers (e.g., Synergy Rehab, Norton Children's) require separate records requests for each companion even though they treated at the same facility. (5) Provider records rejection due to incomplete authorization (missing DOB, missing signature date) was observed in multiple cases. (6) EMS provider records were requested from Louisville Metro EMS, Medical Center Ambulance Services, Okolona EMS, Fern Creek EMS — each requiring separate requests. (7) "No records found" responses from Norton Women's and Children's Hospital required re-routing to Norton Children's Hospital.
    57|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed existing workflow: (1) records/billing requests sent via fax and email within the first 1-2 weeks of file setup; (2) requests directed to university hospital systems (UofL Health), community hospitals, EMS providers, and chiropractic offices; (3) UofL Health records returned via ROI department with confirmation ("Received" from ROI tech); (4) hospital records and bills sometimes received on separate dates (e.g., records on day 9, bills on day 10 from same provider); (5) EMS records required separate fax request to EMS provider (Louisville Metro EMS, Anchorage EMS); (6) one case had only a single minimal-treatment provider (walk-in clinic, $99 bill) — records and bills were received together within 9 days; (7) in a slip-and-fall case managed by a third-party claims administrator, records from a chiropractic provider revealed that treatment was related to a prior MVA and not the slip-and-fall incident — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute; (8) PIP carriers (State Farm, Shelter Insurance) were sent records/billing requests in parallel with provider requests, with the standard language "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (9) in 3 companion cases, records were collected independently for each client even when they shared the same accident and some of the same providers.
    58|
    59|## Agent Guidance
    60|
    61|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    62|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    63|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    64|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    65|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    66|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    67|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    68|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    69|
    70|## Pitfalls
    71|
    72|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    73|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    74|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    75|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    76|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    77|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    78|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    79|
    80|## Related
    81|
    82|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    83|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    84|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    85|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    86|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    87|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase
