     2|title: PIP Claims Management
     3|slug: pip-claims-management
     4|phase: [phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [pip, insurance, bills, medical-payments, ledger, kentucky]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 132
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-bill-routing-workflow
    13|  - pip-ledger-requests
    14|  - treatment-gap-defense
    15|  - va-lien-management
    16|  - multi-carrier-negotiation
    17|---
    18|
    19|# PIP Claims Management
    20|
    21|> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|PIP management follows this lifecycle:
    26|
    27|1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
    28|2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
    29|3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
    30|4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
    31|5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid
    32|
    33|Key observations from case data:
    34|- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
    35|- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
    36|- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
    37|- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
    38|- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
    43|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
    44|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
    45|- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
    46|- In Case D, PIP was used to pay a VA lien directly. The firm sent a "direction to pay" the VA lien ($4,437.13) to the PIP carrier along with the VA billing statement. The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs. This demonstrates PIP's utility beyond paying medical providers — it can satisfy government liens.
    47|- In the same case, the PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was "related to a condition she experienced years prior" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness." PIP adjusters perform their own relatedness analysis and will deny bills they consider unrelated to the accident.
    48|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), two separate PIP claims were set up: one with Farmers Insurance (client's own carrier) and one with American Transit Insurance (NYC cab company involved in a separate incident). The PIP application for Farmers was received and returned in early Dec 2022. The case illustrates managing PIP claims across multiple carriers when there are concurrent accidents, requiring careful tracking of which bills are attributable to which accident for PIP purposes.
    49|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), PIP was managed through Allstate Insurance, the client's own carrier. The PIP adjuster was contacted separately from the UIM adjuster at the same carrier (different adjuster and claim number). The firm requested the ER bill copy from PIP and saved acknowledgment with the PIP log. This demonstrates the pattern where PIP and UIM are both through the same carrier but managed on separate tracks.
    50|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), a Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) claim was set up through Nationwide PIP. The KAC process applies when the at-fault driver has no insurance (because coverage was denied due to the stolen vehicle). The PIP adjuster from Nationwide (Beth Freeman) managed the KAC claim, which followed a different process than standard PIP — requiring the firm to submit specific documentation and the adjuster to coordinate with other parties regarding billing disputes. The attorney agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC for payment, showing that the KAC process involves negotiation about which bills are covered.
    51|- In 13 cases from this batch (all KY MVAs, DOIs 4/2025 to 12/2025), PIP claims were set up with major carriers including State Farm, Progressive, Root Auto, Kentucky Farm Bureau, National General, Elco Insurance, Direct Auto Insurance, and SafeCo. Key patterns confirmed: (1) PIP LOR is sent on day one via fax/email to the PIP carrier; (2) PIP bills are submitted as they are received from providers with the standard request "please process the attached bill"; (3) PIP ledger requests are a recurring task — "please forward an updated PIP ledger" or "please forward a copy of the most recent PIP ledger" was observed in 8+ cases; (4) Same-carrier PIP and BI (e.g., Progressive handling both PIP and BI on separate claim numbers) requires careful claim number tracking; (5) In one case, the paralegal asked the PIP carrier to "process any remaining bills from [provider]" indicating batch bill submission at treatment completion.
    52|- PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
    53|- In a companion case involving 4 family members (2 adults, 2 minor children), the PIP adjuster completed the coverage investigation and communicated detailed payment decisions in a single email covering all four claimants: $4,000 reserved per person, specific billing codes denied per claimant (e.g., 97010 Hot/Cold Packs, 97014 E-stim, 97110 Therapeutic Exercise for some; 97140 Manual Therapy and 97110 for others), and ER bills excluded "per your direction." This is the most detailed PIP adjuster communication observed — showing that PIP adjusters perform individual line-item billing analysis and that the firm can direct PIP to exclude certain bill categories (such as ER bills).
    54|- In the same companion case, the PIP adjuster issued Reservation of Rights letters for the minor children because "proof of loss" could not be confirmed through the police report and claimant statement alone. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as alternate proof of loss. This demonstrates that PIP coverage for passengers (especially minors) may require additional verification beyond the police report.
    55|- In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
    56|- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
    57|- In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
    58|- In 8 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), PIP management was observed across State Farm (4 cases), Progressive (1), Liberty Mutual (1), National General (1), and Root Auto Insurance (1). Key observations: (1) PIP exhaustion triggers provider billing inquiries — in one case, a chiropractic/urgent care provider emailed requesting a PIP log and asking "please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement"; the firm responded with a PIP exhaust letter; (2) In one case, PIP was confirmed as "plenty of PIP remaining" and the firm instructed the provider to resubmit billing directly to PIP; (3) State Farm PIP was managed through a centralized PIP team (separate from the BI adjuster) accessible via a dedicated phone extension; (4) One case with Root Auto Insurance as PIP carrier and a different carrier for BI confirmed the pattern of split-carrier PIP/BI management; (5) In one case, the PIP carrier (Progressive) requested recorded statements as part of a "proof of loss" investigation for PIP claims, scheduling it through the firm — this is distinct from the BI recorded statement and was handled as a separate event.
    59|- In one case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "NO PIP COVERAGE" at intake on the same day. The case proceeded with BI only (Travelers Insurance). This confirms that not all KY auto accident cases have PIP, and the coverage determination must be made immediately at intake. This is the second observed no-PIP case in the wiki.
    60|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    65|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    66|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    67|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    68|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    69|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    70|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    71|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    76|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    77|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    78|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    79|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    80|
    81|## Related
    82|
    83|- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
    84|- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
    85|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
    86|", "total_lines": 85, "file_size": 12344, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Health Insurance Lien Management
     3|slug: lien-management-health-insurance
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation, phase_6_lien_resolution]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [lien, health-insurance, subrogation, anthem, negotiation, pre-existing]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 82
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - pip-claims-management
    13|  - demand-value-factors
    14|  - pre-existing-condition-separation
    15|  - va-lien-management
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    19|
    20|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    27|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    28|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    29|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    30|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    31|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    32|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    37|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA with 20+ providers), the case accumulated 6+ liens including health insurance (Optum at $1,707), provider liens (Cutting Edge Orthopedics at $25,763.75), and a hospital funding lien (Plano Surgical Hospital Funding LLC at $2,553.70). Multiple "Unknown" lien holders were also identified, illustrating that lien tracking becomes exponentially harder with more providers. Provider liens (as distinct from health insurance liens) may come from providers who treated on a lien basis.
    38|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid both asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm used ZipLiens to track the health insurance lien but initially sent the request to the wrong entity. Delta Dental of Kentucky also asserted a lien (amount unconfirmed). This demonstrates that dental injury cases can involve dental insurance liens in addition to health insurance liens — a distinct lien type not seen in standard MVA cases.
    39|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), Humana asserted a health insurance subrogation lien of $11,809. The case also involved pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners), illustrating how the combination of health insurance liens plus funding can dramatically reduce client net recovery.
    40|- In Case H (auto accident, Medicaid), a Conduent lien of $20,669.08 was identified. Conduent is the third-party administrator for Kentucky Medicaid subrogation. The firm sent a first lien letter to Conduent at intake and later provided the Conduent lien information to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. Conduent called the firm to follow up on the case status, demonstrating that Medicaid subrogation administrators actively monitor cases.
    41|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), multiple health insurance liens were identified early: Anthem BCBS, a managed care provider, and a pain management provider lien. The lien request was faxed to the health insurer on the day of file setup. Additionally, multiple "Unknown" lien holders appeared in the lien tracker, demonstrating that in cases with 6+ medical providers, identifying all subrogation interests takes extended effort. The case's policy limits (25/50 split among 4 plaintiffs) made lien resolution particularly critical since the per-plaintiff recovery was limited.
    42|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), a health insurance lien was identified from Humana at $5,887.97 (amount confirmed). Three additional "Unknown" lien holders were also listed. The Humana lien was identified early in the case via the case summary. The confirmed lien amount combined with the estate nature of the case (deceased client) means lien resolution will impact the surviving spouse's net recovery. This case further confirms the pattern that health insurance liens must be identified at intake and tracked throughout the case — even in litigation that spans 5+ years.
    43|- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY), a union benefit trust (TeamCare) asserted a health insurance lien of $89,212.31. A second lien from a BCBS plan was also identified. The TeamCare lien required calling the trust's main line (800 number) and escalating when the member's status was flagged as "not active." The firm spoke with trust representatives multiple times before the status was being reinstated. The lien verification service Zipliens was used to confirm amounts. This case illustrates that union/multi-employer trust benefit plan liens can be among the largest health insurance liens observed, and that trust plan administrative issues (inactive member status) can create delays in lien confirmation.
    44|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY), multiple liens were identified including a hospital lien from a Medicaid managed care plan ($607.41 confirmed), plus two "Unknown" lien holders and a health insurance lien from a third-party administrator. The multiplicity of unknown liens confirms the pattern that in cases with Medicaid managed care, multiple subrogation entities may be involved (the MCO itself, the state Medicaid program, and third-party administrators).
    45|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), a Medicaid lien of $2,452.30 was confirmed from a state Medicaid managed care plan. An additional "Unknown" health insurance lien was also present. Despite extensive treatment ($176K+ in medical bills), the Medicaid lien was relatively modest — confirming that Medicaid reimbursement rates result in smaller liens relative to total billing.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    50|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    51|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    52|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    53|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    54|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    55|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    56|
    57|## Pitfalls
    58|
    59|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    60|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    61|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    62|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    63|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    64|
    65|### Government vs. Private Liens
    66|
    67|Note: Government entity liens (VA, Medicare, Medicaid) follow fundamentally different processes than private health insurance liens. See [[va-lien-management]] for the VA-specific lien process. Key differences include federal legal authority, longer timelines, and less flexibility in negotiation.
    68|
    69|## Related
    70|
    71|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    72|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    73|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
    74|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA liens are a distinct category requiring different handling
    75|
    76|### Batch 3 Evidence (6 additional cases)
    77|
    78|- In 5+ cases, ZipLiens was used as the standard lien verification and resolution service. ZipLiens processed Medicaid liens, Anthem/Carelon liens, and sent automated notifications when liens were processed. The firm relied on ZipLiens for lien identification, amount verification, and negotiation.
    79|- In a case (DOI 2/2023, KY), after settlement the Anthem lien payment of $4,664.54 was mailed by check from the firm. The lien was tracked by Anthem file number.
    80|- In a case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the Medicaid lien payment of $2,526.69 was mailed to Carelon (Anthem's subrogation entity). Carelon subsequently reached out for a "status update on the distribution of the settlement funds" when the payment had not been received, and the firm's ZipLiens file remained open.
    81|- In a case (DOI 5/2022, KY), Optum called asking for an update on lien payment after PIP was exhausted. The firm asked the analyst to send the updated lien with reduction — confirming that lien amounts are negotiated downward post-settlement.
    82|- In a settled case (DOI 11/2023, KY), med pay from the at-fault carrier's policy was used specifically to cover the health insurance lien: "Settled at $15,000. Need to use Med Pay from policy to cover health lien." This confirms med pay as a strategic tool for lien resolution.
    83|- In a multi-track case, the BI carrier specified "settled inclusive of all liens" vs "exclusive of PIP" — these settlement terms directly affect how liens are allocated in the disbursement.
    84|", "total_lines": 83, "file_size": 10088, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    14|---
    15|
    16|# Statute of Limitations Monitoring
    17|
    18|> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.
    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|### Batch 2 Evidence (10 additional cases)
    23|
    24|- In 10+ cases from this batch, automated SOL countdown alerts were observed in Filevine, firing at milestones: 180 days, 90 days, 60 days, 30 days, 20 days, 15 days, 10 days, then daily from 9 days down to 0 and past SOL (-120 days observed). In one case (DOI 12/2023), the full countdown sequence from 180 days through -120 days was visible, indicating the SOL passed without resolution — the case remained open with the SOL having expired.
    25|- In a case taken over from prior counsel (DOI 6/2022), the firm urgently needed the PIP ledger to "confirm SOL" — the prior firm's timeline was unclear, and the 2-year Kentucky SOL was approaching. This demonstrates that SOL verification is a critical intake step when acquiring cases from other firms.
    26|- In 2 cases (DOI 3/2024, DOI 12/2023), SOL alerts were still counting down at -10 days (10 days until SOL), indicating the cases were approaching SOL without settlement — creating pressure to either accept current offers or file suit.
    27|- The SOL countdown alerts are fully automated and appear as Filevine-generated entries in the activity log. They are not acknowledgeable — they fire regardless of case status. Strikethrough formatting (~~) indicates dismissed/acknowledged alerts.
    28|
    29|SOL monitoring follows a countdown pattern with escalation triggers:
    30|
    31|- **180 days out**: Initial alert — ensure case is actively being worked
    32|- **90 days out**: Elevated alert — settlement or suit filing decision must be imminent
    33|- **30 days out**: Critical alert — attorney must decide: file suit, accept offer, or take other action
    34|
    35|Cases that are still in negotiation as the SOL approaches create high-risk situations. The firm must either:
    36|1. Reach settlement before the SOL expires
    37|2. File a lawsuit to preserve the claim (can still settle after filing)
    38|3. Obtain a tolling agreement from the defense
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case C, the SOL countdown appeared in the activity log: "30 day(s) to SOL in Case Summary (as of 3/24/2026)" with the actual SOL date of 4/23/2026 (exactly 2 years from the 4/23/2024 accident date). Prior alerts at 180 and 90 days were also logged and struck through as passed.
    43|- In Case E (auto accident, DOI 2/5/2023), the SOL countdown appeared in the activity log on 8/9/2024: "5 day(s) to SOL in Case Summary (as of 1/31/2025)." The SOL date of 2/5/2025 (exactly 2 years from the accident) was approaching while the case was in demand/negotiation phase. The BI adjuster proactively called around the demand preparation time to check on status — adjusters may be aware of SOL timing and use it in their strategy.
    44|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA, DOI 11/08/2022), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year after the accident (case number 23-CI-005509), well before the 2-year SOL. Filing suit preserved the claim and allowed litigation to proceed while negotiations with a second carrier continued. The pre-emptive filing illustrates the pattern of using litigation as leverage when initial negotiation offers are unreasonably low.
    45|- In Case F (auto accident, DOI 4/7/2023), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court (25-CI-000116) approximately 22 months after the accident. The case involved prolonged dental treatment that delayed the demand process, and the firm filed before the 2-year SOL expired.
    46|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), a UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580) approximately 14 months after the accident. The UIM complaint was filed against the client's own insurer (Allstate) separately from the BI claim against the at-fault driver's insurer (State Farm). A courtesy copy was sent to the UIM adjuster.
    47|- In Case H (auto accident, DOI 4/16/2021), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court (22-CI-002878) approximately 13 months after the accident, well before the 2-year SOL. The early filing was driven by coverage denial on the BI claim (insurer denied coverage due to vehicle theft claim), requiring litigation to force coverage resolution.
    48|- In a declined auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the Filevine SOL countdown continued running even after the case was moved to "Closing" status. Alerts counted down from 180 days through negative days (-30 days past SOL), demonstrating that automated SOL alerts are not suppressed when a case is closed/declined. The SOL expired at 3/6/2026 (exactly 2 years from accident). This confirms that SOL monitoring is purely automated and does not account for case status — staff must manually recognize when alerts on closed cases can be ignored.
    49|- In a WC+BI case (DOI 6/2021), the firm received the case referral from a TN attorney the day before the 2-year SOL expired. The managing attorney emailed: "New client must file suit by tomorrow." A complaint was drafted and e-filed in the same day (Henry Circuit Court 23-CI-00096). The case then moved directly to Litigation phase, skipping File Setup, Treatment, and Demand. This is the most extreme SOL pattern: emergency same-day filing with no prior case development.
    50|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), SOL alerts fired at both 180 days ("180 day(s) to 8/6/2026") and 90 days ("90 day(s) to [SOL] as of 3/1/2026"). Records and bills were still outstanding. The case had not yet sent a demand. This creates the risk of being forced into litigation before the case is fully developed.
    51|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the SOL alert showed "180 day(s) to 8/6/2026" — at which point the client was still in dental treatment and records were being collected. The 2-year KY SOL was approaching while the case was genuinely not ready for demand.
    52|- In a UM/litigation MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the case was in litigation against the client's own carrier (UM claim against Allstate). The SOL had been preserved by complaint filing, but the case lingered in discovery for months with defense counsel corresponding about scheduling. Even with suit filed, cases can stall in litigation without resolution pressure.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. Calculate the SOL date at intake and enter it in the case management system
    57|2. Verify the SOL calculation: in Kentucky, PI is 2 years from date of accident
    58|3. At 180 days out, flag the case to the attorney with current status
    59|4. At 90 days out, escalate: what is the plan for resolution?
    60|5. At 30 days out, the attorney must make a decision: settle, file suit, or obtain tolling agreement
    61|6. Never let a case approach SOL without attorney awareness
    62|7. For multi-state cases, verify the correct jurisdiction's SOL (it may differ)
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Miscalculating the SOL date (especially for out-of-state accidents)
    67|- Relying solely on automated alerts without manual verification
    68|- Allowing a case to stall in negotiation as SOL approaches
    69|- Not escalating to the attorney early enough to prepare a complaint if needed
    70|- Assuming the SOL can be extended without a formal agreement
    71|
    72|## Related
    73|
    74|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — SOL creates hard deadlines for phase progression
    75|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — SOL approach is a mandatory attorney review trigger
    76|", "total_lines": 75, "file_size": 7891, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/negotiation-after-demand.md", "old_string": "- In 4+ cases, the adjuster's formal settlement email included specific conditions: "exclusive of PIP subrogation claim," "inclusive of all other claims including Medicare/Medicaid," "hold harmless/indemnity." The "exclusive of PIP" language remains the standard in Kentucky settlements.", "new_string": "- In 4+ cases, the adjuster's formal settlement email included specific conditions: "exclusive of PIP subrogation claim," "inclusive of all other claims including Medicare/Medicaid," "hold harmless/indemnity." The "exclusive of PIP" language remains the standard in Kentucky settlements.

### Batch 5 Evidence (8 supplementary cases)

- In a multi-plaintiff MVA case (DOI 6/2021, KY, minor child with facial scar), a detailed multi-round negotiation was documented between attorney and Indiana Farm Bureau adjuster. The adjuster's initial offer was $2,500 pain-and-suffering for the minor's soft tissue claim, plus offered to pay medical ($3,703.22) through med pay. The firm countered at $75,000 (citing facial scar on a child). The adjuster replied that she had "room to move, but not to limits" and requested a counter demand. This case demonstrates that scar/disfigurement claims on minors — especially facial scars — are valued dramatically higher by the firm than standard soft-tissue claims, and adjusters expect counter-demands rather than "remaining limits" as a blanket response.
- In a case (DOI 3/2021, KY), the adjuster (American Family) called back during settlement to flag a child support lien discovered in the file, asking about its status. This confirms that adjusters independently identify child support liens during settlement processing, which can complicate disbursement.
- In a multi-party MVA case (DOI 2/2022, KY, litigation), co-plaintiff counsel (separate firm) and the at-fault carrier (Progressive) agreed to mediate the split of a $50K policy among multiple plaintiffs. The adjuster "offered to pay to mediate." The firm's attorney questioned whether the case should simply be resolved with the at-fault carrier rather than pursuing UIM against Allstate (which was dismissed). This shows a novel pattern: the at-fault carrier offering to fund mediation for inter-plaintiff allocation disputes."}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/settlement-disbursement-workflow.md", "old_string": "- Post-settlement provider payment was documented: funeral home providers calling months after settlement requesting remaining balance payment, confirming that post-settlement lien/provider issues can persist for months.", "new_string": "- Post-settlement provider payment was documented: funeral home providers calling months after settlement requesting remaining balance payment, confirming that post-settlement lien/provider issues can persist for months.

### Batch 5 Evidence (6 supplementary cases)

- In a minor's MVA settlement (DOI 6/2021, KY), the disbursement required a blocked bank account at a local bank (Peoples Bank). The bank sent account documents (agreement, disclosures, W-9, online banking forms) that required the guardian's signature. The bank then required proof of address when the guardian's address didn't match her ID — adding a round-trip delay. The firm and bank exchanged follow-up emails over 3+ months, with bank staff turnover causing further delays. The guardian was difficult to reach (phone number changed, "Stop" text received). This confirms that minor settlement disbursement through blocked accounts introduces a distinct multi-party bottleneck: firm <-> bank <-> guardian, each capable of stalling the process.
- In a settled MVA case (DOI 8/2021, KY), the client disputed the settlement check amount at pick-up, stating her agreement was to receive a specific net amount "in her pocket." The check reflected 40% attorney fee but the client believed she was promised a different amount. The paralegal escalated: "even if we reduced fee to 33.3% — only brings her [less than expected]." This demonstrates a fee-agreement dispute at the moment of disbursement — a risk when the client's recollection of fee terms differs from the signed agreement.
- In a settled MVA case (DOI 2/2021, KY), the client left multiple voicemails over weeks asking about her settlement check, saying she was "getting worried." The check was ultimately mailed with tracking. The wait between "settlement agreed" and "check in hand" was several weeks — the client's frustration was driven by the attorney being unavailable to sign the check during that window."}

 - In 29 cases, VineSign was used for release signing, confirming universal VineSign usage for settlement documents. Release completion was logged with timestamps and signer details.
 - The release-before-check pattern continues: the BI carrier requires "release signed and returned to me prior to making payment" across all carriers observed.
 - Post-settlement provider payment was documented: funeral home providers calling months after settlement requesting remaining balance payment, confirming that post-settlement lien/provider issues can persist for months.
+

### Batch 5 Evidence (5 supplementary cases)

- In a passenger MVA case (DOI 1/2022, KY), PIP was through the vehicle owner's policy (Shelter Insurance), not the client's own policy. The firm sent PIP LOR on day 2 of intake and simultaneously requested a complete PIP file copy including "copies of all records and bills received to date and an updated payment ledger." A UIM claim was also opened with the client's own carrier (Nationwide) — confirming that when the client is a passenger, PIP comes from the vehicle owner's policy while UIM comes from the client's own policy.
- In a self-employed client's MVA case (DOI 2/2021, KY), the PIP application was complicated: the unsigned PIP app had to be resent, and the PIP carrier (Kentucky Farm Bureau) asked whether the client was "in the process of employment when MVA happened" because WC would be primary if so. The paralegal clarified the client was self-employed (doing eyelashes). The PIP carrier also delayed lost wage reimbursement, with the client calling repeatedly about not receiving lost wages. This confirms that self-employment status creates PIP processing complications for both medical bill payment and lost wages.
- In a multi-party MVA case (DOI 7/2021, KY), the PIP was through Auto Owners Insurance with a dedicated adjuster. The firm requested PIP log repeatedly ("Have we received PIP log from Auto Owners Insurance?"). The PIP track ran in parallel with a $50K Progressive BI policy tender and a Workers' Compensation claim through Sedgwick — confirming three-track parallel management (PIP + BI + WC) on a single case."}

### Batch 5 Evidence (2 supplementary cases)

- In a passenger MVA case (DOI 1/2022, KY), the full SOL countdown sequence was documented from 180 days down to -180 days past the 1/25/2024 SOL date. The case appears to have passed its 2-year SOL while still in an unresolved state — the SOL countdown alerts continued firing with strikethrough formatting as each milestone passed. Notably, the case involved PIP through Shelter Insurance (vehicle owner's policy) with medical bills still going to collections even after the SOL passed, with the client calling about unpaid bills a year after the SOL expired. This confirms that SOL expiration does not automatically close PIP or medical billing tracks — those obligations persist independently.
- In a litigated MVA case (DOI 2/2022, KY), the case was filed as a lawsuit and subsequently moved from Litigation to Settlement phase in June 2025 — over 3 years post-accident. The litigation filing preserved the claim well before the 2-year SOL. This confirms the standard pattern of filing suit to preserve claims when pre-suit negotiation is unlikely to resolve the case."}

function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import os, re, yaml

def parse_frontmatter(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()
    m = re.match(r'^---\n(.*?)\n---', content, re.DOTALL)
    if not m:
        return None
    try:
        return yaml.safe_load(m.group(1))
    except:
        return None

articles = []
for subdir in ['concepts', 'connections']:
    dirpath = os.path.join('.', subdir)
    if not os.path.isdir(dirpath):
        continue
    for fname in sorted(os.listdir(dirpath)):
        if not fname.endswith('.md'):
            continue
        path = os.path.join(subdir, fname)
        fm = parse_frontmatter(path)
        if fm:
            fm['_path'] = path
            fm['_subdir'] = subdir
            articles.append(fm)

concepts = [a for a in articles if a['_subdir'] == 'concepts']
connections = [a for a in articles if a['_subdir'] == 'connections']

phase_names = {
    'phase_0_onboarding': 'Phase 0: Onboarding',
    'phase_1_file_setup': 'Phase 1: File Setup',
    'phase_2_treatment': 'Phase 2: Treatment',
    'phase_3_demand': 'Phase 3: Demand',
    'phase_4_negotiation': 'Phase 4: Negotiation',
    'phase_5_settlement': 'Phase 5: Settlement',
    'phase_6_lien_resolution': 'Phase 6: Lien Resolution',
    'phase_7_litigation': 'Phase 7: Litigation',
    'phase_8_closed': 'Phase 8: Closed',
}

phase_map = {}
for a in articles:
    phases = a.get('phase', [])
    for p in phases:
        if p not in phase_map:
            phase_map[p] = []
        phase_map[p].append(a)

cat_map = {}
for a in articles:
    cat = a.get('category', 'uncategorized')
    if cat not in cat_map:
        cat_map[cat] = []
    cat_map[cat].append(a)

lines = []
lines.append(f"# Law Firm Wiki — Index\n")
lines.append(f"Last compiled: 2026-04-12 | Articles: {len(concepts)} | Connections: {len(connections)}\n")
lines.append("## By Phase\n")

for phase_key in sorted(phase_names.keys()):
    name = phase_names[phase_key]
    items = phase_map.get(phase_key, [])
    items.sort(key=lambda a: a['_path'])
    lines.append(f"### {name}")
    lines.append("| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |")
    lines.append("|---------|----------|------------|----------|")
    for a in items:
        lines.append(f"| [[{a['_path'].replace('.md','')}]] | {a.get('category','?')} | {a.get('confidence','?')} | {a.get('evidence_count','?')} |")
    lines.append("")

lines.append("## By Category\n")
cat_display = {
    'adjuster-tactics': 'Adjuster Tactics',
    'communication': 'Communication',
    'edge-cases': 'Edge Cases',
    'financial': 'Financial',
    'legal': 'Legal',
    'medical': 'Medical',
    'process': 'Process',
    'connection': 'Connection',
    'phase-knowledge': 'Phase Knowledge',
    'tools': 'Tools',
}

for cat_key in sorted(cat_map.keys()):
    cat_name = cat_display.get(cat_key, cat_key.title())
    items = cat_map[cat_key]
    items.sort(key=lambda a: a['_path'])
    lines.append(f"### {cat_name}")
    lines.append("| Article | Phase | Confidence |")
    lines.append("|---------|-------|------------|")
    for a in items:
        phases = a.get('phase', [])
        phase_nums = []
        for p in phases:
            m = re.search(r'(\d)', p)
            if m:
                phase_nums.append(m.group(1))
        phase_str = ','.join(phase_nums)
        lines.append(f"| [[{a['_path'].replace('.md','')}]] | {phase_str} | {a.get('confidence','?')} |")
    lines.append("")

lines.append("## Recent Updates\n")
lines.append("| Date | Article | Action |")
lines.append("|------|---------|--------|")

# Get recent updates - all existing ones plus our new updates
updated_articles = [
    ('2026-04-12', 'negotiation-after-demand', 'updated (batch 5)'),
    ('2026-04-12', 'settlement-disbursement-workflow', 'updated (batch 5)'),
    ('2026-04-12', 'medical-records-collection', 'updated (batch 5)'),
    ('2026-04-12', 'pip-claims-management', 'updated (batch 5)'),
    ('2026-04-12', 'lien-management-health-insurance', 'updated (batch 5)'),
    ('2026-04-12', 'statute-of-limitations-monitoring', 'updated (batch 5)'),
]

# Also include all existing created articles
all_slugs = sorted(set(a.get('slug','') for a in articles))
for slug in all_slugs:
    lines.append(f"| 2026-04-12 | {slug} | created |")

for date, slug, action in updated_articles:
    lines.append(f"| {date} | {slug} | {action} |")

output = '\n'.join(lines) + '\n'
with open('index.md', 'w') as f:
    f.write(output)
print(f"Index written: {len(concepts)} concepts, {len(connections)} connections")
PYEOF", "timeout": 15}
